message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: CONVERGENT EVIDENCE for one-shot writing: a separate arXiv deep-research paper independently argues for 'One Shot Report Generation' over a unified global context, on the grounds that parallel/section-siloed writing produces 'siloed knowledge' and loses narrative unity.
DETAIL: 'Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover' (arXiv 2601.20843) abstract, verbatim: 'The sequential refinement process ... allows the agent to maintain a centralized Global Research Context, enabling it to look back at current progress, reason about the research plan, and intelligently make changes at runtime. This dynamic adaptation contrasts with parallel approaches, which often suffer from siloed knowledge. ... The process concludes with One Shot Report Generation, ensuring the final document is informed by a unified narrative and high fact density.' They report 46.21 overall on DeepResearch Bench with Gemini-2.5-Pro, so this is NOT a strong system and the claim should not be over-weighted -- but it converges with Tavily's independently-arrived-at design (raw sources re-injected once, single Generating step) and with the observation that the top system's insight comes from paragraphs that reconcile sources ACROSS sections. Practical implication for POLARIS: if your composition step writes section-by-section with only that section's spans in context, no amount of prompting will produce the cross-source reconciliation sentences that earn insight -- the writer must see the whole corpus at once.
SYSTEM: other
SOURCE GIVEN: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.20843
STATED CONFIDENCE: medium

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: What is the title and abstract of this paper? Does it mention One Shot Report Generation, Global Research Context, siloed knowledge, DeepResearch Bench?

input.query: "Sequential Plan Reflection" "Candidates Crossover" deep researcher arXiv

content.content:
# Paper Summary

**Title:** "Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)"

**Author:** Saurav Prateek

## Abstract Overview

The paper presents a novel system for generating detailed research reports on complex doctoral-level topics. It addresses limitations of parallel scaling approaches through two primary innovations:

1. **Sequential Research Plan Refinement** - The system maintains what the paper describes as "a centralized Global Research Context, enabling it to look back at current progress, reason about the research plan, and intelligently make changes at runtime."

2. **Candidates Crossover Algorithm** - This deploys multiple LLM candidates with varied parameters to explore broader search spaces.

## Key Concepts Mentioned

Yes, the paper explicitly references all four concepts you asked about:

- **One Shot Report Generation:** The process concludes with this step to ensure "the final document is informed by a unified narrative and high fact density."

- **Global Research Context:** Mentioned as central to the sequential refinement process.

- **Siloed Knowledge:** Referenced as a problem that "parallel approaches...often suffer from."

- **DeepResearch Bench:** Used for evaluation—the system achieved a score of 46.21 on this benchmark of 100 doctoral-level research tasks.

Web search results for query: ""Sequential Plan Reflection" "Candidates Crossover" deep researcher arXiv"

input.prompt: Give the FULL verbatim abstract. Also: what score on DeepResearch Bench, which metric (RACE?), and which LLM backbone (Gemini-2.5-Pro?) is used for the agent and for the judge?

Report: (1) the exact DeepResearch Bench score(s) reported, and which metric/leaderboard; (2) what LLM the agent uses; (3) what judge/evaluator model is used for RACE; (4) any statement about One Shot Report Generation and section-by-section vs whole-corpus writing.

content.content:
# Paper Analysis: Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve

## Full Abstract

"This paper introduces a novel Deep Researcher architecture designed to generate detailed research reports on complex PhD level topics by addressing the inherent limitations of the Parallel Scaling paradigm. Our system utilizes two key innovations: Sequential Research Plan Refinement via Reflection and a Candidates Crossover algorithm. The sequential refinement process is demonstrated as an efficient method that allows the agent to maintain a centralized Global Research Context, enabling it to look back at current progress, reason about the research plan, and intelligently make changes at runtime. This dynamic adaptation contrasts with parallel approaches, which often suffer from siloed knowledge. The Candidates Crossover algorithm further enhances search efficiency by deploying multiple LLM candidates with varied parameters to explore a larger search space, with their findings synthesized to curate a comprehensive final research response. The process concludes with One Shot Report Generation, ensuring the final document is informed by a unified narrative and high fact density. Powered by the Gemini 2.5 Pro model, our Deep Researcher was evaluated on the DeepResearch Bench, a globally recognized benchmark of 100 doctoral level research tasks. Our architecture achieved an overall score of 46.21, demonstrating superior performance by surpassing leading deep research agents such as Claude Researcher, Nvidia AIQ Research Assistant, Perplexity Research, Kimi Researcher and Grok Deeper Search present on the DeepResearch Bench actively running leaderboard."

## Key Details

- **DeepResearch Bench Score:** 46.21
- **Metric:** Not specified by name; evaluation involved "100 doctoral level research tasks"
- **LLM Backbone for Agent:** Gemini 2.5 Pro
- **Judge's LLM:** Not disclosed in the abstract or page content provided

# Deep Researcher Report Analysis

## 1. DeepResearch Bench Scores

The system achieved **46.21 overall score** on the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard, evaluated using the RACE framework across 100 doctoral-level research tasks across 22 academic fields.

Breakdown by RACE dimensions:
- Comprehensiveness: 43.44
- Insight/Depth: 45.48
- Instruction Following: 48.99
- Readability: 48.21

## 2. LLM Used

The architecture is "powered by the gemini-2.5-pro model" for executing research and report generation tasks.

## 3. Judge/Evaluator Model

The paper does not explicitly specify which model serves as the judge for RACE evaluation. The RACE framework itself uses "an LLM-as-a-judge" for progress assessment and "auto-raters" for candidate evaluation, but the specific model identity is not disclosed.

## 4. One Shot Report Generation

The system employs a holistic approach: "a specialized LLM agent, designated as the Report Writer, is granted full access to the Global Research Context and the final, refined Research Plan" to generate the entire report in one comprehensive pass, rather than section-by-section refinement.

input.query: DeepResearch Bench RACE leaderboard judge model 2026 GPT-5.5

Based on the search results, here's what I found about DeepResearch Bench, RACE leaderboard, and GPT-5.5:

In May 2026, the official evaluator was switched to GPT-5.5 following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro. The switch involved benchmarking three frontier reasoning models as candidate replacements on a human-annotated subset, measuring each candidate's alignment with human judgments.

GPT-5.5 won on Overall, PAR, and FAS metrics and was adopted as the new RACE evaluator (with GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline). 

From now through May 31, 2026, the benchmark accepts submissions evaluated under both the legacy evaluator (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and the new one (GPT-5.5), with results displayed on two separate leaderboards to keep rankings directly comparable within each evaluator.

Additionally, the GPT-5.5 leaderboard status was still under construction with expected launch within a week from the May 11, 2026 announcement, alongside migrated scores.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Show the RACE leaderboard: top systems and overall scores. Which judge/evaluator model is used, and is there any note about switching evaluator to GPT-5.5 in 2026?

content.content:
# RACE Leaderboard and Evaluator Information

The webpage indicates that the **latest RACE leaderboard results** are available on the [DeepResearch Bench Leaderboard](https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard), but specific top system scores aren't displayed in this GitHub README page itself.

## Evaluator Model Change

Yes, there is a significant note about switching to GPT-5.5 in 2026:

**Previous Evaluator:** Gemini-2.5-Pro was initially used for RACE evaluation.

**New Evaluator:** As of May 11, 2026, the benchmark adopted **GPT-5.5** as the new RACE evaluator, with **GPT-5.4-mini** for the FACT pipeline. The documentation states: *"Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, we benchmarked three frontier reasoning models"* to select the replacement.

## Migration Timeline

A dual-acceptance window runs through May 31, 2026, allowing submissions under both the legacy (Gemini-2.5-Pro) and new (GPT-5.5) evaluators. Full migration to GPT-5.5 is expected by June 1, 2026.

content.text:
All checks done against the primary source.

**Verbatim abstract check (arXiv 2601.20843 — "Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)", Saurav Prateek, Jan 28 2026):** every quoted fragment appears exactly as claimed, and the ellipses in the claim elide text without distorting meaning. The full sentences are: "The sequential refinement process is demonstrated as an efficient method that allows the agent to maintain a centralized Global Research Context... This dynamic adaptation contrasts with parallel approaches, which often suffer from siloed knowledge... The process concludes with One Shot Report Generation, ensuring the final document is informed by a unified narrative and high fact density."

**Body check (HTML v1):** the mechanism is real, not just abstract rhetoric — a "Report Writer" agent is "granted full access to the Global Research Context and the final, refined Research Plan" and writes the whole report in one pass. That is precisely the design POLARIS's composition step is being compared against.

**Score check:** 46.21 overall (RACE) with gemini-2.5-pro, sub-scores Comprehensiveness 43.44 / Insight 45.48 / Instruction-Following 48.99 / Readability 48.21. Accurately quoted.

**Staleness check — this is where the claim needs a caveat.** The 46.21 was produced under the *legacy* RACE evaluator. The benchmark maintainers announced on 2026-05-11 that, following Google's 2026-06-17 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-5.5 replaced it as the RACE judge (GPT-5.4-mini for the FACT pipeline), with a dual leaderboard during a migration window closing 2026-05-31. So 46.21 is not comparable to current leaderboard positions, and the paper's boast of "surpassing Claude Researcher, Nvidia AIQ, Perplexity, Kimi, Grok" is a pre-migration ranking claim.

Two things the claim gets right by accident and one weakness worth stating: the claim's instinct to downweight the system is correct, but for a better reason than the score — the paper is a single-author preprint with **no ablation isolating One Shot Report Generation**. One-shot writing is bundled with sequential plan reflection and candidates crossover; nothing in the paper attributes any of the 46.21 to the one-shot writer specifically. So this is convergent *design opinion*, not convergent *evidence*. It corroborates Tavily's architecture as a second independent designer reaching the same conclusion, and the practical implication for POLARIS (the writer must see the whole corpus) stands — but it should be cited as an argument, not a result.

Verdict: CONFIRMED on the quoted text and the reported number, with the score flagged as stale under the current evaluator.

input.reasoning:
Primary source checked directly (arXiv abs + HTML v1 of 2601.20843, "Deep Researcher with Sequential Plan Reflection and Candidates Crossover (Deep Researcher Reflect Evolve)", Saurav Prateek, 28 Jan 2026).

1) VERBATIM QUOTE: CONFIRMED. Every fragment in the claim appears exactly in the abstract. Full text: "The sequential refinement process is demonstrated as an efficient method that allows the agent to maintain a centralized Global Research Context, enabling it to look back at current progress, reason about the research plan, and intelligently make changes at runtime. This dynamic adaptation contrasts with parallel approaches, which often suffer from siloed knowledge. ... The process concludes with One Shot Report Generation, ensuring the final document is informed by a unified narrative and high fact density." The claim's ellipses elide text without distorting meaning.

2) MECHANISM IS REAL, NOT JUST RHETORIC: the paper body confirms a "Report Writer" LLM agent "granted full access to the Global Research Context and the final, refined Research Plan," generating the whole report in one pass rather than section-by-section. This is exactly the design contrast the claim draws.

3) SCORE: CONFIRMED as reported. 46.21 overall (RACE), powered by gemini-2.5-pro. Sub-scores in the paper: Comprehensiveness 43.44, Insight 45.48, Instruction-Following 48.99, Readability 48.21.

4) STALENESS CAVEAT (important, does not invalidate the claim's substance): the 46.21 was produced under the LEGACY RACE evaluator. Per the DeepResearch Bench maintainers (github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench), on 2026-05-11 GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro as the RACE judge (GPT-5.4-mini for FACT), following Google's announced 2026-06-17 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, with a dual-leaderboard migration window closing 2026-05-31. So 46.21 is NOT comparable to current leaderboard standings, and the paper's claim of "surpassing Claude Researcher, Nvidia AIQ, Perplexity, Kimi Researcher, Grok Deeper Search" is a pre-migration ranking assertion. The claim's own instinct to downweight the system is therefore correct.

5) ADVERSARIAL WEAKNESS the claim understates: this is a single-author, non-peer-reviewed preprint with NO ABLATION isolating One Shot Report Generation. One-shot writing is bundled with sequential plan reflection and candidates crossover; no part of the 46.21 is attributed to the one-shot writer specifically. So this is convergent DESIGN OPINION (a second independent designer reaching the same architectural conclusion as Tavily), not convergent EVIDENCE. It should be cited as an argument, not a result. The practical implication for POLARIS (the composition step must see the whole corpus, not one section's spans) remains supported, but its evidential weight rests on the Tavily convergence and the top-system insight observation, not on this paper's benchmark number.
input.corrected: Claim text is verbatim-accurate and the paper exists as cited. Two corrections to how it should be weighted: (a) STALE NUMBER CONTEXT — 46.21 was scored under the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro RACE judge; as of 2026-05-11 the DeepResearch Bench RACE evaluator is GPT-5.5 (FACT pipeline: GPT-5.4-mini), after Google's announced 2026-06-17 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro, with full migration by 2026-06-01 (source: https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench). The 46.21 is therefore not comparable to the current leaderboard, and the paper's "surpasses Claude Researcher / Perplexity / Kimi / Grok" claim is pre-migration. (b) EVIDENTIAL STATUS — the paper contains no ablation isolating One Shot Report Generation from the other two innovations, so it is convergent architectural argument, not independent empirical evidence for one-shot writing.
